results.possibleIdentity: Ron Cooper (born 1943) is an American visual artist associated with the Light and Space and Finish Fetish movements of Southern California. Active since the mid-1960s, his work explores light, color, and perception using industrial materials such as fluorescent light, neon, glass, and metal. Cooper was a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) exhibition titled "Some More Beginnings," held at the Brooklyn Museum in 1968, which showcased collaborations between artists and engineers. He was also identified in a 1969 Esquire article as one of Andy Warhol’s "Dirty Half Dozen" associated with the radical intermedia and "New Theatre" scene, alongside artists like Carolee Schneemann and Yayoi Kusama. In the late 1960s, his studio served as a venue for experimental activities, including dance workshops featuring collaborators like Alex Hay. Outside of his visual arts career, Cooper is well-known as the founder of Del Maguey Single Village Mezcal, which he established in 1995.

results.possibleIdentity: Peter Ballantine (often spelled Balentine in historical inquiries) is a master woodworker, sculptor, and fabricator who was active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his close professional association with the minimalist artist Donald Judd and the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1972, Judd hired Ballantine, a former student of the Whitney Independent Study Program, to perform renovation work on his building at 101 Spring Street; Ballantine subsequently became the primary fabricator for Judd’s signature plywood works and furniture. His expertise was considered so integral to the work that Judd's certificates of authenticity often specified that Ballantine held the construction information or was the only person capable of making certain plywood pieces. Ballantine is also significantly linked to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through the 'New York Collection for Stockholm' project (1971–1973). He designed and built the original Honduras mahogany boxes used to house the print portfolios for this collection, which featured works by thirty major New York artists. In later decades, Ballantine has served as a curator and scholar of Donald Judd's work, organizing exhibitions such as 'Donald Judd' at the Mnuchin Gallery in 2009.
